nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving forest streams, ponds, and pools richly carpeted with leaf litter, fallen branches, and organic debris; typically shallow, tannin-stained blackwater environments

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate 5–8 cm deep for burrowing
dried leaf litter (oak or beech leaves) for camouflage and cover
bogwood and driftwood providing shaded resting spots
dim lighting or floating plants to diffuse light
low flow — still or very gentle current

diet.notes: Nocturnal micro-predator and opportunistic bottom-feeder. Primarily accepts live and frozen invertebrates: bloodworm, white worm, Tubifex, mosquito larvae, small earthworms, and brine shrimp. Also consumes small benthic invertebrates and organic detritus. Rarely accepts dry foods unaided; sinking carnivore wafers or pellets may be taken once the fish is settled. Feed after lights-out; competition at feeding time from faster tankmates is a common cause of weight loss. Avoid copper-containing preparations.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember tetras, neon tetras, rummy-nose tetras)
dwarf cichlids (Apistogramma spp.) that do not disturb the bottom
avoidWith: boisterous or fast-feeding fish that out-compete at feeding time
fish treated with copper-based medications in a shared tank
very small fry or nano shrimp (may be ingested opportunistically)

imageCandidates.notes: Dorsal view showing characteristic banjo/leaf-like body shape; good detail of mottled camouflage patterning.

summary: A remarkably camouflaged Amazon bottom-dweller whose flattened, mottled body mimics a dead leaf. Strictly nocturnal and sedentary by day; a rewarding oddity for the patient intermediate keeper who can meet its blackwater and target-feeding needs.
careNotes:
The Banjo Catfish (Bunocephalus coracoideus) is one of the most distinctive freshwater catfishes available to aquarists — its dorsoventrally flattened body, rough scaleless skin, and mottled brown coloration combine to produce near-perfect dead-leaf mimicry. The species is scaleless and must be treated as copper-sensitive; never use copper-based medications in any tank housing this fish. A sand substrate at least 5 cm deep is essential: the fish buries itself during daylight hours and is rarely seen outside dusk to dawn. Leaf litter (dried oak or beech leaves) and bogwood replicate its Amazonian forest-stream biotope and provide additional cover.

Tank minimum is approximately 75 L (60 cm footprint) for a small group; SeriouslyFish cite a 71 L / 60 cm tank as adequate. Soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 5.8–7.8, gH 2–20) mirrors its blackwater native range. Temperature 20–27 °C is well-tolerated; the middle-to-upper portion of that range (24–27 °C) is preferred for active feeding.

Feeding is the primary care challenge. The fish is strictly nocturnal and will not compete at community feeding time; all foods must be offered after lights-out or via targeted bottom-feeding. Live and frozen invertebrates (bloodworm, Tubifex, white worm, mosquito larvae) are the staples. Sinking wafers may be accepted once the fish acclimates. Underfeeding due to competition is the most common husbandry failure. Keeping three or more individuals together increases activity and natural behaviour without significant aggression.

Taxonomic note: fw-fish-262 (Bunocephalus coracoideus) is the same species as fw-fish-261 (formerly listed as Dysichthys coracoideus). Friel’s 1994 doctoral revision of Aspredinidae synonymized the genus Dysichthys into Bunocephalus; Bunocephalus coracoideus (Cope, 1874) is the current valid name accepted by FishBase, Catalog of Fishes, and SeriouslyFish. These two entries will be merged in end-of-slice cleanup.
breedingNotes: Rarely accomplished in home aquaria. At least six mature individuals are required; soft, warm water and leaf-litter-covered sand substrate replicate spawning conditions. Spawning occurs nocturnally on the substrate. Eggs are reported to hatch in approximately three days; FishBase notes clutch sizes of 4,000–5,000 eggs per event. Fry accept microworm and baby brine shrimp nauplii. Unlike many Aspredinidae relatives (which carry eggs attached to the belly), B. coracoideus does not practice egg-carrying — eggs are deposited on the substrate and left without parental care. No reliably repeatable captive-breeding protocol is established in the hobby literature.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Bunocephalus coracoideus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (max 15 cm SL), temperature (20–27 °C), pH (5.8–7.8), hardness (2–20 °H), minimum tank (71 L / 60 cm), native range (Bolivia, Peru, Brazil), substrate requirements, feeding behaviour, breeding notes, and compatibility guidance. Taxonomy: Family Aspredinidae, Subfamily Bunocephalinae.

additional.name: FishBase — Bunocephalus coracoideus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (11.0 cm SL), temperature (25–28 °C), pH (6.0–8.0), native range (Amazon River basin, South America), family Aspredinidae, order Siluriformes. Notes benthic habitat in ponds and forest creeks with plant debris; minimum aquarium length 80 cm cited. FishBase size 11 cm SL vs SeriouslyFish 15 cm — SeriouslyFish figure used as conservative trade maximum.

additional.name: Fishkeeping Secrets — Banjo Catfish Care
additional.notes: Confirmed lifespan 5–8 years in captivity, temperature 22–28 °C, pH 6.5–7.5, gH 5–19, copper sensitivity, minimum group size, and compatible tankmates. Noted feeding-competition risk from faster tankmates as primary care failure point.

additional.name: Catalog of Fishes (CAS) — Bunocephalus coracoideus
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority. Confirms Bunocephalus coracoideus (Cope, 1874) as the valid name; Dysichthys coracoideus listed as junior synonym following Friel’s Aspredinidae revision.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — 4 Unusual Catfish Suitable for a Community Aquarium
additional.notes: Supplementary trade source; confirmed banjo catfish availability in the hobby, leaf-mimicry camouflage, and community compatibility notes. Specific parameters deferred to SeriouslyFish as primary.

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity; requires group of 6+, soft warm water, and leaf-litter substrate. Eggs (~4,000–5,000 per spawn) deposited on substrate and not guarded; hatch in ~3 days. Fry accept microworm and BBS. No established repeatable protocol in hobby literature.
